title: 1 Chronicles 2
subtitle: The Sons of Israel; Judah’s Line to David

sections.header: Verses 1–17 — The Twelve Sons; Judah’s Line Through Perez

refs.note: “The sceptre will not depart from Judah.” The Chronicler’s emphasis on Judah’s genealogy reflects this patriarchal blessing.
The Perez-to-David genealogy at the end of Ruth matches 1 Chr 2:5–16 exactly. Both texts establish the Davidic line through Perez.

notes.note: The priority of Judah teaches that God’s purposes follow his own logic, not human expectations. The firstborn (Reuben) is passed over; the fourth son (Judah) carries the promise. Divine election overrides natural order. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The numbering of David as the “seventh” differs from 1 Sam 16:10–11 and 17:12 (which imply eight sons of Jesse). Various explanations include an early death of one brother or different counting traditions. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: The twelve sons of Israel are listed, then the focus narrows sharply to Judah. The line runs through Perez (born from Judah and Tamar) to Hezron to Ram to Jesse to David. The Chronicler is building a genealogical highway from Israel to the throne. Judah receives more genealogical attention than any other tribe — 55 verses in this chapter alone — because Judah carries the royal promise (Gen 49:10).

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew tôlĕḏôt (generations) is a structuring device that traces the thread of God's purpose through family lines. From Genesis to Chronicles, genealogies are not mere lists but theological arguments: they demonstrate continuity, establish legitimacy, and show that God's covenant promises pass from generation to generation.

notes.note: The Chronicler records this history not merely as political narrative but as theological instruction. Every event demonstrates God's sovereign governance of His people and His unwavering faithfulness to the Davidic covenant. The believing reader is meant to see in these events the pattern of God's dealings with His church in every age.

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

refs.note: Caleb’s allotment in Judah’s territory. The Chronicler traces Caleb’s descendants through their settlements, connecting people to places.

notes.note: The inclusion of Jarha the Egyptian within Judah’s genealogy teaches that God’s covenant community has always absorbed outsiders. The genealogy is not a wall but a gate — open to those who join Israel. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The “Kenites who came from Hammath” are linked to Recab, ancestor of the Recabites (Jer 35). Their inclusion within Judah’s genealogy reflects long-standing alliance and assimilation. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: The genealogy expands into the sub-clans of Hezron — Caleb, Jerahmeel, and others — tracing the settlement patterns of Judahite families in the hill country. These verses read like a tribal land registry: family names are attached to towns and territories. For the post-exilic community, this material answers the practical question: which families belong to which territories? The genealogy is a property deed dressed as a family tree.

heb.paragraph: Mišpāḥâ (clan/family) is the intermediate social unit between the household (bayit) and the tribe (šēḇeṭ). In the genealogical sections, clan identity determines inheritance, military obligation, and cultic responsibility. The Chronicler's careful attention to clan structures reflects the post-exilic community's concern for legitimate succession.

notes.note: The Chronicler records this history not merely as political narrative but as theological instruction. Every event demonstrates God's sovereign governance of His people and His unwavering faithfulness to the Davidic covenant. The believing reader is meant to see in these events the pattern of God's dealings with His church in every age.

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe sons of Judah: Er, Onan and Shelah. These three were born to him by a Canaanite woman, the daughter of Shua. (Er, Judah’s firstborn, was wicked in the Lord’s sight; so the Lord put him to death.)ESVThe sons of Judah: Er, Onan and Shelah; these three Bath-shua the Canaanite bore to him. Now Er, Judah’s firstborn, was evil in the sight of the LORD, and he put him to death.NIVThe sons born to Hezron were: Jerahmeel, Ram and Caleb.ESVThe sons of Hezron that were born to him: Jerahmeel, Ram, and Chelubai.

themes.note: The Sons of Israel; Judah’s Line to David

coaching.tip: The genealogy traces Judah's line first — before the other tribes — because the Chronicler is building toward David. The genealogical order is theological, not chronological. Judah leads because the Davidic dynasty matters most to the post-exilic community.
